quotes: Innate immune cells, such as macrophages and innate lymphoid cells (ILCs), were present from early gestation, whereas B cells and T cells emerged later, accompanying thymus, bone marrow and spleen formation from around 10 PCW
Single-cell alpha-beta T cell receptor (TCR) sequencing data were generated to accurately resolve T cell subsets
prenatal skin matrix cells had increased expression of genes involved in chemotaxis, such as CXCL14, a chemokine previously reported to recruit regulatory T (Treg) cells
T reg cells are known to localize around the HF in late second trimester (around 21 PCW) and in postnatal skin
summary: The atlas paper establishes that T cells (including CD4+ T cells) emerge in prenatal skin from around 10 PCW, later than innate immune cells. TCR sequencing was used for T cell subset resolution. Treg cells are noted to localize around hair follicles, and CXCL14 from matrix cells may recruit Tregs during hair follicle development.
source_title: A prenatal skin atlas reveals immune regulation of human skin morphogenesis
snippet: atlas paper body text and extended data

quotes: prenatal conventional T cells (CD4+ T, CD8+ T, and Tregs), whereas resident and effector memory T cells did not have a developmental equivalent
T cell progenitors were restricted to the thymus, potentially reflecting more stringent niche requirements for T cell development
Conventional CD4+ T and CD8+ T cells colocalized with mTECs close to the inner medulla
summary: Suo et al. mapped prenatal conventional T cell populations including CD4+ T, CD8+ T, and Tregs across organs, finding that prenatal T cells could match adult naive counterparts but not resident/effector memory T cells. T cell progenitors were restricted to the thymus. In the thymus, conventional CD4+ and CD8+ T cells colocalized with medullary thymic epithelial cells.
source_title: Mapping the developing human immune system across organs

quotes: the largest subsets of both CD4+ Tconv and CD8+ T cells in fetal skin demonstrated a largely naive, proliferative status, whereas those dominating in adult skin were enriched for markers of memory and activation
CD4+ (clusters 1 and 2) and CD8+ (cluster 4) T cells represented the bulk of lymphocytes present in fetal skin
Strikingly, up to 30% of CD4+ Tconv and slightly fewer CD8+ T cells in fetal skin were CD45RO+
CD31+ T cells were substantially enriched in fetal skin at both 17-18 and 23 weeks
the average percentage of Foxp3+ regulatory T cells (Tregs) among CD4+ cells increased significantly between 17 and 23 weeks gestation and was higher at this later fetal time point than in adult skin

Accumulation of Tregs in fetal skin occurs contemporaneously with hair follicle development, and preferentially at sites of higher hair follicle density within a given fetus, suggesting an early role for hair follicle biology in skin Treg residency.
summary: Dhariwala et al. provided deep phenotyping of CD4+ T cells in human fetal skin using CyTOF and flow cytometry. Most fetal CD4+ Tconv cells had a naive, proliferative phenotype (high Ki-67, CD27; low CD25, CD45RO), distinct from adult memory/activated counterparts. However, a subset (~30%) expressed CD45RO, indicating memory-like features. CD31 expression indicated enrichment for recent thymic emigrants. Foxp3+ Tregs progressively increased during the second trimester, coinciding with hair follicle development.
source_title: Developing Human Skin Contains Lymphocytes Demonstrating a Memory Signature

quotes: the majority of CD3+ cells expressed CD4, thus, confirming previous results described by Di Nuzzo et al. (2009)
FoxP3 expression was found in 20.0% (SD 6.5%; n=5) of all T cells, a frequency comparable to what is found in the adult skin
the majority of skin-homing T cells in fetal skin are naive
summary: Schuster et al. characterized leukocytes in prenatal human dermis, finding that the majority of CD3+ T cells in fetal skin expressed CD4. FoxP3+ regulatory T cells were present at ~20% of T cells, comparable to adult skin. Fetal skin T cells were predominantly naive.
source_title: Phenotypic Characterization of Leukocytes in Prenatal Human Dermis
